== References == * Kikuo Arai, "Shinabe" ("Encyclopedia of Japanese History 3" (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1) * Masaaki Ueda, "Shinabe, Zodo" (Encyclopedia of Social Sciences 14 (Kajima Institute Press, 1974) ISBN 978-4-306-09165-8) * {{Cite book|author=Jinno Seiichi|title=Genealogy of the View of Low and Highborn|date=1997-02-01|publisher={{Ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}} |isbn=4-642-05408-1|series=History and Culture Library|volume=8|location =[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language=Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jino}}}} {{Cite book|author=Jino Seiichi| title=Genealogy of the Low Countries View|date=1997-02-01|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|isbn=4-642-05408-1|series="History and Culture Library"|volume=8|location=[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language= Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jono}}}} * Kimio Kumagai, "Pinabe" and "Pinabe and Zodo System" in Dictionary of National History 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986) ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4
[[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese tribes]] [[Category:Shinabe clans]] [[Category:Bemin]]

{{More footnotes needed|date=October 2021}}
{{Nihongo|Shinabe|品部}} were a caste in the [[Yamato kingship]]. It is a type of [[Bemin]] clan which is dedicated to a specific occupation rather than farming.<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,百科事典マイペディア,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,世界大百科事典 |title=品部(ともべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%93%81%E9%83%A8-74567 |access-date=2023-01-28 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> They paid tribute to and served the Yamato government during the [[Kofun period|pre-Taika period]] in Japan. They lived in various places and were placed under the jurisdiction of Tomomo-zukuri (Tomomo-no-miyazuko). They were sometimes called by the name of their tribute or occupation, and sometimes by the name of the banzo in charge of them. When the banzo was powerful, they were considered to be private citizens. Under the decree system, they were liberated and most of them became civilians.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=第２版,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,百科事典マイペディア,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,世界大百科事典 |title=品部(ともべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%93%81%E9%83%A8-74567 |access-date=2023-01-29 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
According to legend, they descended from [[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |date=24 February 2006 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenoakarutama |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9437 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211024102555/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9437 |archive-date=24 October 2021 |access-date=19 December 2020 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]]}}</ref>
== History == In the pre-Taika period, they were led by powerful clans such as the Banzo, and provided various goods and labor to the Imperial Court (Yamato Kingdom). It is also said that many of the groups of engineers who came to Japan after the latter half of the [[5th century]] belonged to the Shinabe clan. After the [[Taika Reform]], some of them were abolished, but the rest were reorganized and attached to government officials, who were obliged to produce luxury goods for the court and industrial products requiring special technology as part of tribute. In the staff ordinances within the [[Taihō Code|Daihō ordinances]], the affiliated government offices, the name of the product department, and the legal number of units were each defined as follows.
* Library - paper door (50) * Gagaku-ryo - Gagaku (49), Kioto (8), Nara-fueki (9) * Zouheiji - Zoukou - Zoukoudo...Tsume-kou (18), Tatehou (36), Bunbaku (16) * Drum-blowers - drum-blowing doors (large angle blowers) (218) * Chusenji - Funado (Funamorido) (100) * Main falconer - falconer's door (falconry door) (17) * Okura-sho - Komainoto...Oshikaido Komainoto (5), Takeshido Komainoto (7), Murakami Komainoto (30), Miyagun Komainoto (14), Dai Komainome (6), Koromoome (21), Asuka Kutsumi (12), Kuretokosaku (2), Futaumi (11), Okasami (33), Suddenly Saku (72) * Lacquer Master - Lacquer Department...Lacquer (15), Claywork (10), Leatherwork (4) * Weaving: Nishiki Twill Weaving (110), Kofuku Department (7), Kawauchi Kunihiro Silk Weavers, etc. (350), Scarlet Dyeing (70), Indigo Dyeing (33) * [[Ōzenshoku]] - Zoukido...Ukai (37), Ejin (87), Amibiki (150), Mishou (20) * [[Ōiryō|Otsukuryo]]-Otsukurido (25) * [[Ōiryō|Otakiryo]]-Yakudo (75), Nyudo (50) * [[Zōzakeji]]-Sakeido (185) * Sonokoji - Sonoto (300) * Earthworks - mud (51) * Shusuiji - Hido (Mito) (114)
As for their status, they were considered to be good citizens like ordinary citizens (peasants), but their status was slightly inferior to that of ordinary citizens. However, they were less subordinate to the government, and were registered in the family register of their place of residence on the same basis as ordinary citizens, so there was almost no discrimination in treatment between them and ordinary citizens. In addition to the regular members who inherited their duties and status, there were also temporary members who were incorporated from the general public. In other cases, one person per household either worked in shifts for a certain period of time, or was temporarily assigned by the government to perform labor or pay a certain amount of tribute. In exchange, they were exempted from some or all of their household duties and were also excluded from military service.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
In the [[Nara period]], in [[Yōrō]] 5 ([[721]]), the chief hawks were abolished, and in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 3 ([[759]]), all hinto except for highly skilled "sekkyo-sōdensha" were, in principle, abolished and incorporated into the public domain. In [[759]], in principle, Shinto houses other than those for highly skilled "sekkyo-soden" were abolished and incorporated into public houses (treated as ordinary citizens). In the [[Heian period]], only the Kofukishi of the Hyogo Dormitory were included in the [[Engishiki|Enki shiki]]. This is thought to have been due to a complicated combination of factors: the fact that the old tribal system itself was incompatible with the Ritsuryo system; the fact that the decline of the Ritsuryo system made it difficult to maintain the tribal system; and the fact that the improvement of socioeconomic standards made it possible to procure and hire services from the private sector (in this case, the private sector included those procured from the former tribal merchants and traders). In this case, the private sector included the commercial and industrial sectors of the former Shinbu.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}
In academic circles, it is sometimes referred to as ''Shinabe Zakkosei'' together with the similar organization of Zakkosei, but Zakkosei were engaged in military-related technical labor and had their own family registers that were different from the general family registers, and the legal status difference between them and the public was stronger than that of Shinabe. The legal status difference between them and the public is said to be stronger than that of the Shinabe.
== Clans ==
=== {{Anchor|Tobu clan}}Tobu clan === The term {{Nihongo|2=陶部|3=Tōbu}} refers to a [[shinabe clan]] in ancient Japan who were engaged in the production of ceramics, specifically {{Nihongo|2=須恵|3=Sue}} ware. According to the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), Emperor Yūryaku ordered the migration of skilled ceramic workers from the Korean peninsula to Japan in the 7th century. The production of Sue ware continued to be carried out by descendant clans of these immigrants until the Taika Reforms of the 7th century, after which the Tōbu became common citizens. However, some Tōbu continued to be registered as Hinbu (品部) or Zatō (雑戸), which were lower-ranking classes under the central government. There is no record of the Tōbu being under the direct control of the Imperial Household Agency.<ref>{{Cite web |last=三訂版,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,精選版 日本国語大辞典,旺文社日本史事典 |title=陶部(すえべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%99%B6%E9%83%A8-83354 |access-date=2023-01-26 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
=== {{Anchor|Fuhibote|Fuhito clan}}Fuhito clan === The Fuhitobe ('''史部)''', were a caste in [[Japan]] during the [[Kofun period]]. They were a [[Shinabe clan]] who were engaged in duties related to writing at the [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato Imperial Court]]. Most of them were thought to be descended from [[Toraijin]] or people who came to Japan from the continent or peninsula. Unlike the general nobles, they were given the family name "Fuhito" (史).<ref name=":03">https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8F%B2%E9%83%A8</ref>
The duties of the Fuhitobe included taking charge of records and documents during the period before the [[Taika (era)|Taika era]] (618-867).<ref name=":03" />
In the Chinese context, the term "Shibu" of the same Kanji ('''史部)''' is also used to refer to one of the four divisions of literature, known as the Shi division, which includes historical books and geography.<ref name=":03" /> <ref>{{Cite web |title=“史部”包括哪些典籍？ – 国学网 |url=http://www.guoxue.com/?p=221 |access-date=2023-01-27 |language=zh-CN}}</ref>

After the [[Taika Reform]], some of them were abolished, but the rest were reorganized and attached to government officials, who were obliged to produce luxury goods for the court and industrial products requiring special technology as part of tribute. In the staff ordinances within the [[Taihō Code|Taihō ordinances]], the affiliated government offices, the name of the product department, and the legal number of units were each defined as follows.
* Library - paper door (50) * Gagaku-ryo - Gagaku (49), Kioto (8), Nara-fueki (9) * Zouheiji - Zoukou - Zoukoudo...Tsume-kou (18), Tatehou (36), Bunbaku (16) * Drum-blowers - drum-blowing doors (large angle blowers) (218) * Chusenji - Funado (Funamorido) (100) * Main falconer - falconer's door (falconry door) (17) * Okura-sho - Komainoto...Oshikaido Komainoto (5), Takeshido Komainoto (7), Murakami Komainoto (30), Miyagun Komainoto (14), Dai Komainome (6), Koromoome (21), Asuka Kutsumi (12), Kuretokosaku (2), Futaumi (11), Okasami (33), Suddenly Saku (72) * Lacquer Master - Lacquer Department...Lacquer (15), Claywork (10), Leatherwork (4) * Weaving: Nishiki Twill Weaving (110), Kofuku Department (7), Kawauchi Kunihiro Silk Weavers, etc. (350), Scarlet Dyeing (70), Indigo Dyeing (33) * [[Ōzenshoku]] - Zoukido...Ukai (37), Ejin (87), Amibiki (150), Mishou (20) * [[Ōiryō|Otsukuryo]]-Otsukurido (25) * [[Ōiryō|Otakiryo]]-Yakudo (75), Nyudo (50) * [[Zōzakeji]]-Sakeido (185) * Sonokoji - Sonoto (300) * Earthworks - mud (51) * Shusuiji - Hido (Mito) (114)
As for their status, they were considered to be good citizens like ordinary citizens (peasants), but their status was slightly inferior to that of ordinary citizens. However, they were less subordinate to the government, and were registered in the family register of their place of residence on the same basis as ordinary citizens, so there was almost no discrimination in treatment between them and ordinary citizens. In addition to the regular members who inherited their duties and status, there were also temporary members who were incorporated from the general public. In other cases, one person per household either worked in shifts for a certain period of time, or was temporarily assigned by the government to perform labor or pay a certain amount of tribute. In exchange, they were exempted from some or all of their household duties and were also excluded from military service.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
In the [[Nara period]], in [[Yōrō]] 5 ([[721]]), the chief hawks were abolished, and in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 3 ([[759]]), all hinto except for highly skilled "sekkyo-sōdensha" were, in principle, abolished and incorporated into the public domain. In [[759]], in principle, Shinto houses other than those for highly skilled "sekkyo-soden" were abolished and incorporated into public houses (treated as ordinary citizens). In the [[Heian period]], only the Kofukishi of the Hyogo Dormitory were included in the [[Engishiki|Enkishiki]]. This is thought to have been due to a complicated combination of factors: the fact that the old tribal system itself was incompatible with the Ritsuryo system; the fact that the decline of the Ritsuryo system made it difficult to maintain the tribal system; and the fact that the improvement of socioeconomic standards made it possible to procure and hire services from the private sector (in this case, the private sector included those procured from the former tribal merchants and traders). In this case, the private sector included the commercial and industrial sectors of the former Shinbu.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}
In academic circles, it is sometimes referred to as ''Shinabe Zakkosei'' together with the similar organization of Zakkosei, but Zakkosei were engaged in military-related technical labor and had their own family registers that were different from the general family registers, and the legal status difference between them and the public was stronger than that of Shinabe. The legal status difference between them and the public is said to be stronger than that of the Shinabe.
== Clans ==
=== {{Anchor|Tobu clan}}Tobu clan === The term {{Nihongo|2=陶部|3=Tōbu}} refers to a [[shinabe clan]] in ancient Japan who were engaged in the production of ceramics, specifically {{Nihongo|2=須恵|3=Sue}} ware. According to the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), Emperor Yūryaku ordered the migration of skilled ceramic workers from the Korean peninsula to Japan in the 7th century. The production of Sue ware continued to be carried out by descendant clans of these immigrants until the Taika Reforms of the 7th century, after which the Tōbu became common citizens. However, some Tōbu continued to be registered as Hinbu (品部) or Zatō (雑戸), which were lower-ranking classes under the central government. There is no record of the Tōbu being under the direct control of the Imperial Household Agency.<ref>{{Cite web |last=三訂版,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,精選版 日本国語大辞典,旺文社日本史事典 |title=陶部(すえべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%99%B6%E9%83%A8-83354 |access-date=2023-01-26 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
=== {{Anchor|Fuhibote|Fuhito clan}}Fuhito clan === The Fuhitobe ('''史部)''', were a caste in [[Japan]] during the [[Kofun period]]. They were a [[Shinabe clan]] who were engaged in duties related to writing at the [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato Imperial Court]]. Most of them were thought to be descended from [[Toraijin]] or people who came to Japan from the continent or peninsula. Unlike the general nobles, they were given the family name "Fuhito" (史).<ref name=":03">https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8F%B2%E9%83%A8</ref>
The duties of the Fuhitobe included taking charge of records and documents during the period before the [[Taika (era)|Taika era]] (618-867).<ref name=":03" />
In the Chinese context, the term "Shibu" of the same Kanji ('''史部)''' is also used to refer to one of the four divisions of literature, known as the Shi division, which includes historical books and geography.<ref name=":03" /> <ref>{{Cite web |title=“史部”包括哪些典籍？ – 国学网 |url=http://www.guoxue.com/?p=221 |access-date=2023-01-27 |language=zh-CN}}</ref>

{{More footnotes needed|date=October 2021}}
{{Nihongo|Shinabe|品部}} were a caste in the [[Yamato kingship]]. It is a type of [[Bemin]] clan which is dedicated to a specific occupation rather than farming.<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,百科事典マイペディア,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,世界大百科事典 |title=品部(ともべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%93%81%E9%83%A8-74567 |access-date=2023-01-28 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> They paid tribute to and served the Yamato government during the [[Kofun period|pre-Taika period]] in Japan. They lived in various places and were placed under the jurisdiction of Tomomo-zukuri (Tomomo-no-miyazuko). They were sometimes called by the name of their tribute or occupation, and sometimes by the name of the banzo in charge of them. When the banzo was powerful, they were considered to be private citizens. Under the decree system, they were liberated and most of them became civilians.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=第２版,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,百科事典マイペディア,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,世界大百科事典 |title=品部(ともべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%93%81%E9%83%A8-74567 |access-date=2023-01-29 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
According to legend, they descended from [[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |date=24 February 2006 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenoakarutama |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9437 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211024102555/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9437 |archive-date=24 October 2021 |access-date=19 December 2020 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]]}}</ref>
== History == In the pre-Taika period, they were led by powerful clans such as the Banzo, and provided various goods and labor to the Imperial Court (Yamato Kingdom). It is also said that many of the groups of engineers who came to Japan after the latter half of the [[5th century]] belonged to the Shinabe clan. After the [[Taika Reform]], some of them were abolished, but the rest were reorganized and attached to government officials, who were obliged to produce luxury goods for the court and industrial products requiring special technology as part of tribute. In the staff ordinances within the [[Taihō Code|Taihō ordinances]], the affiliated government offices, the name of the product department, and the legal number of units were each defined as follows.
* Library - paper door (50) * Gagaku-ryo - Gagaku (49), Kioto (8), Nara-fueki (9) * Zouheiji - Zoukou - Zoukoudo...Tsume-kou (18), Tatehou (36), Bunbaku (16) * Drum-blowers - drum-blowing doors (large angle blowers) (218) * Chusenji - Funado (Funamorido) (100) * Main falconer - falconer's door (falconry door) (17) * Okura-sho - Komainoto...Oshikaido Komainoto (5), Takeshido Komainoto (7), Murakami Komainoto (30), Miyagun Komainoto (14), Dai Komainome (6), Koromoome (21), Asuka Kutsumi (12), Kuretokosaku (2), Futaumi (11), Okasami (33), Suddenly Saku (72) * Lacquer Master - Lacquer Department...Lacquer (15), Claywork (10), Leatherwork (4) * Weaving: Nishiki Twill Weaving (110), Kofuku Department (7), Kawauchi Kunihiro Silk Weavers, etc. (350), Scarlet Dyeing (70), Indigo Dyeing (33) * [[Ōzenshoku]] - Zoukido...Ukai (37), Ejin (87), Amibiki (150), Mishou (20) * [[Ōiryō|Otsukuryo]]-Otsukurido (25) * [[Ōiryō|Otakiryo]]-Yakudo (75), Nyudo (50) * [[Zōzakeji]]-Sakeido (185) * Sonokoji - Sonoto (300) * Earthworks - mud (51) * Shusuiji - Hido (Mito) (114)
As for their status, they were considered to be good citizens like ordinary citizens (peasants), but their status was slightly inferior to that of ordinary citizens. However, they were less subordinate to the government, and were registered in the family register of their place of residence on the same basis as ordinary citizens, so there was almost no discrimination in treatment between them and ordinary citizens. In addition to the regular members who inherited their duties and status, there were also temporary members who were incorporated from the general public. In other cases, one person per household either worked in shifts for a certain period of time, or was temporarily assigned by the government to perform labor or pay a certain amount of tribute. In exchange, they were exempted from some or all of their household duties and were also excluded from military service.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
In the [[Nara period]], in [[Yōrō]] 5 ([[721]]), the chief hawks were abolished, and in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 3 ([[759]]), all hinto except for highly skilled "sekkyo-sōdensha" were, in principle, abolished and incorporated into the public domain. In [[759]], in principle, Shinto houses other than those for highly skilled "sekkyo-soden" were abolished and incorporated into public houses (treated as ordinary citizens). In the [[Heian period]], only the Kofukishi of the Hyogo Dormitory were included in the [[Engishiki|Enkishiki]]. This is thought to have been due to a complicated combination of factors: the fact that the old tribal system itself was incompatible with the Ritsuryo system; the fact that the decline of the Ritsuryo system made it difficult to maintain the tribal system; and the fact that the improvement of socioeconomic standards made it possible to procure and hire services from the private sector (in this case, the private sector included those procured from the former tribal merchants and traders). In this case, the private sector included the commercial and industrial sectors of the former Shinbu.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}
In academic circles, it is sometimes referred to as ''Shinabe Zakkosei'' together with the similar organization of Zakkosei, but Zakkosei were engaged in military-related technical labor and had their own family registers that were different from the general family registers, and the legal status difference between them and the public was stronger than that of Shinabe. The legal status difference between them and the public is said to be stronger than that of the Shinabe.

== Bibliography == * Kikuo Arai, "Shinabe" ("Encyclopedia of Japanese History 3" (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1) * Masaaki Ueda, "Shinabe, Zodo" (Encyclopedia of Social Sciences 14 (Kajima Institute Press, 1974) ISBN 978-4-306-09165-8) * {{Cite book|author=Jinno Seiichi|title=Genealogy of the View of Low and Highborn|date=1997-02-01|publisher={{Ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}} |isbn=4-642-05408-1|series=History and Culture Library|volume=8|location =[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language=Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jino}}}} {{Cite book|author=Jino Seiichi| title=Genealogy of the Low Countries View|date=1997-02-01|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|isbn=4-642-05408-1|series="History and Culture Library"|volume=8|location=[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language= Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jono}}}} * Kimio Kumagai, "Pinabe" and "Pinabe and Zodo System" in Dictionary of National History 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986) ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4
[[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Bemin]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese institutions]]

{{More footnotes needed|date=October 2021}}
{{Nihongo|Shinabe|品部}} were a caste in the [[Yamato kingship]]. It is a type of [[Bemin]] clan which is dedicated to a specific occupation rather than farming.<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,百科事典マイペディア,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,世界大百科事典 |title=品部(ともべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%93%81%E9%83%A8-74567 |access-date=2023-01-28 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> They paid tribute to and served the Yamato government during the [[Kofun period|pre-Taika period]] in Japan. They lived in various places and were placed under the jurisdiction of Tomomo-zukuri (Tomomo-no-miyazuko). They were sometimes called by the name of their tribute or occupation, and sometimes by the name of the banzo in charge of them. When the banzo was powerful, they were considered to be private citizens. Under the decree system, they were liberated and most of them became civilians.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=第２版,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,百科事典マイペディア,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,世界大百科事典 |title=品部(ともべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%93%81%E9%83%A8-74567 |access-date=2023-01-29 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
According to legend, they descended from [[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |date=24 February 2006 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenoakarutama |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9437 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211024102555/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9437 |archive-date=24 October 2021 |access-date=19 December 2020 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]]}}</ref>
== History == In the pre-Taika period, they were led by powerful clans such as the Banzo, and provided various goods and labor to the Imperial Court (Yamato Kingdom). It is also said that many of the groups of engineers who came to Japan after the latter half of the [[5th century]] belonged to the Shinabe clan. After the [[Taika Reform]], some of them were abolished, but the rest were reorganized and attached to government officials, who were obliged to produce luxury goods for the court and industrial products requiring special technology as part of tribute. In the staff ordinances within the [[Taihō Code|Taihō ordinances]], the affiliated government offices, the name of the product department, and the legal number of units were each defined as follows.
* Library - paper door (50) * Gagaku-ryo - Gagaku (49), Kioto (8), Nara-fueki (9) * Zouheiji - Zoukou - Zoukoudo...Tsume-kou (18), Tatehou (36), Bunbaku (16) * Drum-blowers - drum-blowing doors (large angle blowers) (218) * Chusenji - Funado (Funamorido) (100) * Main falconer - falconer's door (falconry door) (17) * Okura-sho - Komainoto...Oshikaido Komainoto (5), Takeshido Komainoto (7), Murakami Komainoto (30), Miyagun Komainoto (14), Dai Komainome (6), Koromoome (21), Asuka Kutsumi (12), Kuretokosaku (2), Futaumi (11), Okasami (33), Suddenly Saku (72) * Lacquer Master - Lacquer Department...Lacquer (15), Claywork (10), Leatherwork (4) * Weaving: Nishiki Twill Weaving (110), Kofuku Department (7), Kawauchi Kunihiro Silk Weavers, etc. (350), Scarlet Dyeing (70), Indigo Dyeing (33) * [[Ōzenshoku]] - Zoukido...Ukai (37), Ejin (87), Amibiki (150), Mishou (20) * [[Ōiryō|Otsukuryo]]-Otsukurido (25) * [[Ōiryō|Otakiryo]]-Yakudo (75), Nyudo (50) * [[Zōzakeji]]-Sakeido (185) * Sonokoji - Sonoto (300) * Earthworks - mud (51) * Shusuiji - Hido (Mito) (114)
As for their status, they were considered to be good citizens like ordinary citizens (peasants), but their status was slightly inferior to that of ordinary citizens. However, they were less subordinate to the government, and were registered in the family register of their place of residence on the same basis as ordinary citizens, so there was almost no discrimination in treatment between them and ordinary citizens. In addition to the regular members who inherited their duties and status, there were also temporary members who were incorporated from the general public. In other cases, one person per household either worked in shifts for a certain period of time, or was temporarily assigned by the government to perform labor or pay a certain amount of tribute. In exchange, they were exempted from some or all of their household duties and were also excluded from military service.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
In the [[Nara period]], in [[Yōrō]] 5 ([[721]]), the chief hawks were abolished, and in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 3 ([[759]]), all hinto except for highly skilled "sekkyo-sōdensha" were, in principle, abolished and incorporated into the public domain. In [[759]], in principle, Shinto houses other than those for highly skilled "sekkyo-soden" were abolished and incorporated into public houses (treated as ordinary citizens). In the [[Heian period]], only the Kofukishi of the Hyogo Dormitory were included in the [[Engishiki|Enkishiki]]. This is thought to have been due to a complicated combination of factors: the fact that the old tribal system itself was incompatible with the Ritsuryo system; the fact that the decline of the Ritsuryo system made it difficult to maintain the tribal system; and the fact that the improvement of socioeconomic standards made it possible to procure and hire services from the private sector (in this case, the private sector included those procured from the former tribal merchants and traders). In this case, the private sector included the commercial and industrial sectors of the former Shinbu.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}
In academic circles, it is sometimes referred to as ''Shinabe Zakkosei'' together with the similar organization of Zakkosei, but Zakkosei were engaged in military-related technical labor and had their own family registers that were different from the general family registers, and the legal status difference between them and the public was stronger than that of Shinabe. The legal status difference between them and the public is said to be stronger than that of the Shinabe.
== Clans ==
=== {{Anchor|Tobu clan}}Tobu clan === The term {{Nihongo|2=陶部|3=Tōbu}} refers to a [[shinabe clan]] in ancient Japan who were engaged in the production of ceramics, specifically {{Nihongo|2=須恵|3=Sue}} ware. According to the "Nihon Shoki" (Chronicles of Japan), Emperor Yūryaku ordered the migration of skilled ceramic workers from the Korean peninsula to Japan in the 7th century. The production of Sue ware continued to be carried out by descendant clans of these immigrants until the Taika Reforms of the 7th century, after which the Tōbu became common citizens. However, some Tōbu continued to be registered as Hinbu (品部) or Zatō (雑戸), which were lower-ranking classes under the central government. There is no record of the Tōbu being under the direct control of the Imperial Household Agency.<ref>{{Cite web |last=三訂版,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,精選版 日本国語大辞典,旺文社日本史事典 |title=陶部(すえべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%99%B6%E9%83%A8-83354 |access-date=2023-01-26 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
=== {{Anchor|Fuhibote|Fuhito clan}}Fuhito clan === The Fuhitobe ('''史部)''', were a caste in [[Japan]] during the [[Kofun period]]. They were a [[Shinabe clan]] who were engaged in duties related to writing at the [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato Imperial Court]]. Most of them were thought to be descended from [[Toraijin]] or people who came to Japan from the continent or peninsula. Unlike the general nobles, they were given the family name "Fuhito" (史).<ref name=":03">https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8F%B2%E9%83%A8</ref>
The duties of the Fuhitobe included taking charge of records and documents during the period before the [[Taika (era)|Taika era]] (618-867).<ref name=":03" />
In the Chinese context, the term "Shibu" of the same Kanji ('''史部)''' is also used to refer to one of the four divisions of literature, known as the Shi division, which includes historical books and geography.<ref name=":03" /> <ref>{{Cite web |title=“史部”包括哪些典籍？ – 国学网 |url=http://www.guoxue.com/?p=221 |access-date=2023-01-27 |language=zh-CN}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Kikuo Arai, "Shinabe" ("Encyclopedia of Japanese History 3" (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1) * Masaaki Ueda, "Shinabe, Zodo" (Encyclopedia of Social Sciences 14 (Kajima Institute Press, 1974) ISBN 978-4-306-09165-8) * {{Cite book|author=Jinno Seiichi|title=Genealogy of the View of Low and Highborn|date=1997-02-01|publisher={{Ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}} |isbn=4-642-05408-1|series=History and Culture Library|volume=8|location =[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language=Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jino}}}} {{Cite book|author=Jino Seiichi| title=Genealogy of the Low Countries View|date=1997-02-01|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|isbn=4-642-05408-1|series="History and Culture Library"|volume=8|location=[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language= Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jono}}}} * Kimio Kumagai, "Pinabe" and "Pinabe and Zodo System" in Dictionary of National History 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986) ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4

In other cases, one person per household either worked in shifts for a certain period of time, or was temporarily assigned by the government to perform labor or pay a certain amount of tribute. In exchange, they were exempted from some or all of their household duties and were also excluded from military service.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
In the [[Nara period]], in [[Yōrō]] 5 ([[721]]), the chief hawks were abolished, and in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 3 ([[759]]), all hinto except for highly skilled "sekkyo-sōdensha" were, in principle, abolished and incorporated into the public domain. In [[759]], in principle, Shinto houses other than those for highly skilled "sekkyo-soden" were abolished and incorporated into public houses (treated as ordinary citizens). In the [[Heian period]], only the Kofukishi of the Hyogo Dormitory were included in the [[Engishiki|Enkishiki]]. This is thought to have been due to a complicated combination of factors: the fact that the old tribal system itself was incompatible with the Ritsuryo system; the fact that the decline of the Ritsuryo system made it difficult to maintain the tribal system; and the fact that the improvement of socioeconomic standards made it possible to procure and hire services from the private sector (in this case, the private sector included those procured from the former tribal merchants and traders). In this case, the private sector included the commercial and industrial sectors of the former Shinbu.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}
In academic circles, it is sometimes referred to as ''Shinabe Zakkosei'' together with the similar organization of Zakkosei, but Zakkosei were engaged in military-related technical labor and had their own family registers that were different from the general family registers, and the legal status difference between them and the public was stronger than that of Shinabe. The legal status difference between them and the public is said to be stronger than that of the Shinabe.
== Clans ==
=== {{Anchor|Fuhibote|Fuhito clan}}Fuhito clan === The Fuhitobe ('''史部)''', were a caste in [[Japan]] during the [[Kofun period]]. They were a [[Shinabe clan]] who were engaged in duties related to writing at the [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato Imperial Court]]. Most of them were thought to be descended from [[Toraijin]] or people who came to Japan from the continent or peninsula. Unlike the general nobles, they were given the family name "Fuhito" (史).<ref name=":03">https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8F%B2%E9%83%A8</ref>
The duties of the Fuhitobe included taking charge of records and documents during the period before the [[Taika (era)|Taika era]] (618-867).<ref name=":03" />
In the Chinese context, the term "Shibu" of the same Kanji ('''史部)''' is also used to refer to one of the four divisions of literature, known as the Shi division, which includes historical books and geography.<ref name=":03" /> <ref>{{Cite web |title="史部"包括哪些典籍？ – 国学网 |url=http://www.guoxue.com/?p=221 |access-date=2023-01-27 |language=zh-CN}}</ref>

== Bibliography == * Kikuo Arai, "Shinabe" ("Encyclopedia of Japanese History 3" (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1) * Masaaki Ueda, "Shinabe, Zodo" (Encyclopedia of Social Sciences 14 (Kajima Institute Press, 1974) ISBN 978-4-306-09165-8) * {{Cite book|author=Jinno Seiichi|title=Genealogy of the View of Low and Highborn|date=1997-02-01|publisher={{Ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}} |isbn=4-642-05408-1|series=History and Culture Library|volume=8|location =[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language=Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jino}}}} {{Cite book|author=Jino Seiichi| title=Genealogy of the Low Countries View|date=1997-02-01|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|isbn=4-642-05408-1|series=History and Culture Library|volume=8|location=[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language= Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jono}}}} * Kimio Kumagai, "Pinabe" and "Pinabe and Zodo System" in Dictionary of National History 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986) ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4

'''品部'''（しなべ/ともべ）とは、「しなのとものを」あるいは「とものみやつこ」の[[和訓]]を持つ[[古代]][[日本]]の人的集団・組織のこと。[[部民制]]の後の制度。
== 概要 == *複数の[[部民制|部]]あるいは部全体の総称。 *[[大化]]前代においては、朝廷に直属する部民（職業部・[[名代]]）のこと。 *大化以後及び[[律令制]]において[[官司]]に配属されて宮中で用いる物資の生産・技術伝習にあたった人々。大化前代の[[部民制|職業部]]の後身とされる。
上記のように複数の意味を有する。
大化前代においては、[[伴造]]などの豪族に率いられて朝廷（[[ヤマト王権]]）に様々な物資あるいは労働力の形で奉仕を行った。また、[[5世紀]]後半以後に渡来した[[渡来人]]の技術者集団の中には品部に属した者が多いとされている。[[大化の改新]]以後、一部は廃止されるが残りは再編されて官司に付属され、貢納の一環として宮廷で用いる奢侈品や特殊な技術を必要とする工業製品の生産を義務付けられるようになる。[[大宝律令|大宝令]]内の[[職員令]]においては、所属官庁、品部名、法定戸数はそれぞれ以下のように定められていた。
*[[図書寮]]－紙戸（50） *[[雅楽寮]]－楽戸…伎楽（49）・木登（8）・奈良笛吹（9） *[[造兵司]]－雑工戸…爪工（18）・楯縫（36）・幄作（16） *[[鼓吹司]]－[[鼓吹戸]]（大角吹）（218） *[[主船司]]－船戸（船守戸）（100） *[[主鷹司]]－鷹戸（鷹養戸）（17） *[[大蔵省 (律令制)|大蔵省]]－狛戸…忍海戸狛人（5）・竹志戸狛人（7）・村々狛人（30）・宮郡狛人（14）・大狛染（6）・衣染（21）・飛鳥沓縫（12）・呉床作（2）・蓋縫（11）・大笠縫（33）・桉作（72） *[[漆部司]]－漆部…漆部（15）・泥障（10）・革張（4） *[[織部司]]－染戸…錦綾織（110）・呉服部（7）・川内国広絹織人等（350）・緋染（70）・藍染（33） *[[大膳職]]－雑供戸…鵜飼（37）・江人（87）・網引（150）・未醤（20） *[[大炊寮]]－大炊戸（25） *[[典薬寮]]－薬戸（75）・乳戸（50） *[[造酒司]]－酒戸（185） *[[園池司]]－園戸（300） *[[土工司]]－泥戸（51） *[[主水司]]－氷戸（水戸）（114）
身分としては、一般の公民（百姓）と同じ良民を構成したが、公民と比べると身分は若干劣るものとされた。ただし、官庁への隷属度は弱く、戸籍登録も居住地の戸籍に一般公民と等しく登録されるなど、待遇としては一般公民との差別はほぼ無かったとされる。職務・身分を世襲する'''常の品部'''のほかに、一般公民より臨時に編入する'''借（かり）の品部'''が存在した。狛戸及び染戸の一部は毎年一定量の料物を貢納したが、その他は1戸あたり1名が一定期間の交替制か臨時に官司に上番して労役を行うか一定量の製品を貢納した。その代わりとして戸内の課役の一部もしくは全てが免除され、兵役からも除外された{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
[[奈良時代]]に入ると、[[養老]]5年（[[721年]]）に主鷹司の品部廃止が行われ、続いて[[天平宝字]]3年（[[759年]]）に高度な技術を要する「世業相伝者」以外の品戸は原則廃止されて公戸に編入（一般の公民扱い）された。その後も品戸の廃止が続き、[[平安時代]]の『[[延喜式]]』の時点では兵庫寮の鼓吹司のみとなった。これは古い部民制の残滓である品部そのものが律令制と相容れなかったこと、律令制の衰退によって品部の維持が困難になってきたこと、社会経済水準の向上によって民間からの調達・雇役が可能になったこと（この場合の民間には旧品部の商工業者からの調達を含む）が複雑に絡み合っていたと考えられている{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
学術的には同様の組織である[[雑戸]]と合わせて'''品部・雑戸制'''（しなべ・ざっこせい）とよぶ場合もあるが、雑戸の職掌範囲は軍事関係の技術労働に従事しており、またその戸籍は一般の戸籍とは違う[[雑戸籍]]が独自に構成されており、公民との法的な身分差は品部よりも強いとされる{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}。

{{DEFAULTSORT:しなへ}} [[Category:古墳時代]] [[Category:飛鳥時代]] [[Category:奈良時代]] [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:部民|*しなへ]]

{{More footnotes needed|date=October 2021}}
{{Nihongo|Shinabe|品部}} were a caste in the [[Yamato kingship]]. It is a type of [[Bemin]] clan which is dedicated to a specific occupation rather than farming.<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,百科事典マイペディア,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,世界大百科事典 |title=品部(ともべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%93%81%E9%83%A8-74567 |access-date=2023-01-28 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> They paid tribute to and served the Yamato government during the [[Kofun period|pre-Taika period]] in Japan. They lived in various places and were placed under the jurisdiction of Tomomo-zukuri (Tomomo-no-miyazuko). They were sometimes called by the name of their tribute or occupation, and sometimes by the name of the banzo in charge of them. When the banzo was powerful, they were considered to be private citizens. Under the decree system, they were liberated and most of them became civilians.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=第２版,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,百科事典マイペディア,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,世界大百科事典 |title=品部(ともべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%93%81%E9%83%A8-74567 |access-date=2023-01-29 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
According to legend, they descended from [[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |date=24 February 2006 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenoakarutama |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9437 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211024102555/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9437 |archive-date=24 October 2021 |access-date=19 December 2020 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]]}}</ref>
== History == In the pre-Taika period, they were led by powerful clans such as the Banzo, and provided various goods and labor to the Imperial Court (Yamato Kingdom). It is also said that many of the groups of engineers who came to Japan after the latter half of the [[5th century]] belonged to the Shinabe clan. After the [[Taika Reform]], some of them were abolished, but the rest were reorganized and attached to government officials, who were obliged to produce luxury goods for the court and industrial products requiring special technology as part of tribute. In the staff ordinances within the [[Taihō Code|Taihō ordinances]], the affiliated government offices, the name of the product department, and the legal number of units were each defined as follows.
* Library - paper door (50) * Gagaku-ryo - Gagaku (49), Kioto (8), Nara-fueki (9) * Zouheiji - Zoukou - Zoukoudo...Tsume-kou (18), Tatehou (36), Bunbaku (16) * Drum-blowers - drum-blowing doors (large angle blowers) (218) * Chusenji - Funado (Funamorido) (100) * Main falconer - falconer's door (falconry door) (17) * Okura-sho - Komainoto...Oshikaido Komainoto (5), Takeshido Komainoto (7), Murakami Komainoto (30), Miyagun Komainoto (14), Dai Komainome (6), Koromoome (21), Asuka Kutsumi (12), Kuretokosaku (2), Futaumi (11), Okasami (33), Suddenly Saku (72) * Lacquer Master - Lacquer Department...Lacquer (15), Claywork (10), Leatherwork (4) * Weaving: Nishiki Twill Weaving (110), Kofuku Department (7), Kawauchi Kunihiro Silk Weavers, etc. (350), Scarlet Dyeing (70), Indigo Dyeing (33) * [[Ōzenshoku]] - Zoukido...Ukai (37), Ejin (87), Amibiki (150), Mishou (20) * [[Ōiryō|Otsukuryo]]-Otsukurido (25) * [[Ōiryō|Otakiryo]]-Yakudo (75), Nyudo (50) * [[Zōzakeji]]-Sakeido (185) * Sonokoji - Sonoto (300) * Earthworks - mud (51) * Shusuiji - Hido (Mito) (114)
As for their status, they were considered to be good citizens like ordinary citizens (peasants), but their status was slightly inferior to that of ordinary citizens. However, they were less subordinate to the government, and were registered in the family register of their place of residence on the same basis as ordinary citizens, so there was almost no discrimination in treatment between them and ordinary citizens. In addition to the regular members who inherited their duties and status, there were also temporary members who were incorporated from the general public. In other cases, one person per household either worked in shifts for a certain period of time, or was temporarily assigned by the government to perform labor or pay a certain amount of tribute. In exchange, they were exempted from some or all of their household duties and were also excluded from military service.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
In the [[Nara period]], in [[Yōrō]] 5 ([[721]]), the chief hawks were abolished, and in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 3 ([[759]]), all hinto except for highly skilled "sekkyo-sōdensha" were, in principle, abolished and incorporated into the public domain. In [[759]], in principle, Shinto houses other than those for highly skilled "sekkyo-soden" were abolished and incorporated into public houses (treated as ordinary citizens). In the [[Heian period]], only the Kofukishi of the Hyogo Dormitory were included in the [[Engishiki|Enkishiki]]. This is thought to have been due to a complicated combination of factors: the fact that the old tribal system itself was incompatible with the Ritsuryo system; the fact that the decline of the Ritsuryo system made it difficult to maintain the tribal system; and the fact that the improvement of socioeconomic standards made it possible to procure and hire services from the private sector (in this case, the private sector included those procured from the former tribal merchants and traders). In this case, the private sector included the commercial and industrial sectors of the former Shinbu.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}
In academic circles, it is sometimes referred to as ''Shinabe Zakkosei'' together with the similar organization of Zakkosei, but Zakkosei were engaged in military-related technical labor and had their own family registers that were different from the general family registers, and the legal status difference between them and the public was stronger than that of Shinabe. The legal status difference between them and the public is said to be stronger than that of the Shinabe.

=== {{Anchor|Fuhibote|Fuhito clan}}Fuhito clan === The Fuhitobe ('''史部)''', were a caste in [[Japan]] during the [[Kofun period]]. They were a [[Shinabe clan]] who were engaged in duties related to writing at the [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato Imperial Court]]. Most of them were thought to be descended from [[Toraijin]] or people who came to Japan from the continent or peninsula. Unlike the general nobles, they were given the family name "Fuhito" (史).<ref name=":03">https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8F%B2%E9%83%A8</ref>
The duties of the Fuhitobe included taking charge of records and documents during the period before the [[Taika (era)|Taika era]] (618-867).<ref name=":03" />
In the Chinese context, the term "Shibu" of the same Kanji ('''史部)''' is also used to refer to one of the four divisions of literature, known as the Shi division, which includes historical books and geography.<ref name=":03" /> <ref>{{Cite web |title="史部"包括哪些典籍？ – 国学网 |url=http://www.guoxue.com/?p=221 |access-date=2023-01-27 |language=zh-CN}}</ref>

In the [[Nara period]], in [[Yōrō]] 5 ([[721]]), the chief hawks were abolished, and in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 3 ([[759]]), all hinto except for highly skilled "sekkyo-sōdensha" were, in principle, abolished and incorporated into the public domain. In [[759]], in principle, Shinto houses other than those for highly skilled "sekkyo-soden" were abolished and incorporated into public houses (treated as ordinary citizens). In the [[Heian period]], only the Kofukishi of the Hyogo Dormitory were included in the [[Engishiki|Enkishiki]]. This is thought to have been due to a complicated combination of factors: the fact that the old tribal system itself was incompatible with the Ritsuryo system; the fact that the decline of the Ritsuryo system made it difficult to maintain the tribal system; and the fact that the improvement of socioeconomic standards made it possible to procure and hire services from the private sector (in this case, the private sector included those procured from the former tribal merchants and traders). In this case, the private sector included the commercial and industrial sectors of the former Shinbu.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}
In academic circles, it is sometimes referred to as ''Shinabe Zakkosei'' together with the similar organization of Zakkosei, but Zakkosei were engaged in military-related technical labor and had their own family registers that were different from the general family registers, and the legal status difference between them and the public was stronger than that of Shinabe. The legal status difference between them and the public is said to be stronger than that of the Shinabe.
== Clans ==
=== {{Anchor|Fuhibote|Fuhito clan}}Fuhito clan === The Fuhitobe ('''史部)''', were a caste in [[Japan]] during the [[Kofun period]]. They were a [[Shinabe clan]] who were engaged in duties related to writing at the [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato Imperial Court]]. Most of them were thought to be descended from [[Toraijin]] or people who came to Japan from the continent or peninsula. Unlike the general nobles, they were given the family name "Fuhito" (史).<ref name=":03">{{Cite web |last=字通,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),山川 日本史小辞典 改訂新版,世界大百科事典 第２版,普及版 |title=史部(ふびとべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8F%B2%E9%83%A8-620106 |access-date=2023-12-07 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
The duties of the Fuhitobe included taking charge of records and documents during the period before the [[Taika (era)|Taika era]] (618-867).<ref name=":03" />
In the Chinese context, the term "Shibu" of the same Kanji ('''史部)''' is also used to refer to one of the four divisions of literature, known as the Shi division, which includes historical books and geography.<ref name=":03" /> <ref>{{Cite web |title="史部"包括哪些典籍？ – 国学网 |url=http://www.guoxue.com/?p=221 |access-date=2023-01-27 |language=zh-CN}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Kikuo Arai, "Shinabe" ("Encyclopedia of Japanese History 3" (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1) * Masaaki Ueda, "Shinabe, Zodo" (Encyclopedia of Social Sciences 14 (Kajima Institute Press, 1974) ISBN 978-4-306-09165-8) * {{Cite book|author=Jinno Seiichi|title=Genealogy of the View of Low and Highborn|date=1997-02-01|publisher={{Ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}} |isbn=4-642-05408-1|series=History and Culture Library|volume=8|location =[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language=Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jino}}}} {{Cite book|author=Jino Seiichi| title=Genealogy of the Low Countries View|date=1997-02-01|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|isbn=4-642-05408-1|series=History and Culture Library|volume=8|location=[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language= Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jono}}}} * Kimio Kumagai, "Pinabe" and "Pinabe and Zodo System" in Dictionary of National History 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986) ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4
[[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese institutions]] [[Category:Japanese caste system]]

{{More footnotes needed|date=October 2021}}
{{Nihongo|Shinabe|品部}} were a caste in the [[Yamato kingship]]. It is a type of [[Bemin]] clan which is dedicated to a specific occupation rather than farming.<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,百科事典マイペディア,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,世界大百科事典 |title=品部(ともべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%93%81%E9%83%A8-74567 |access-date=2023-01-28 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> They paid tribute to and served the Yamato government during the [[Kofun period|pre-Taika period]] in Japan. They lived in various places and were placed under the jurisdiction of Tomomo-zukuri (Tomomo-no-miyazuko). They were sometimes called by the name of their tribute or occupation, and sometimes by the name of the banzo in charge of them. When the banzo was powerful, they were considered to be private citizens. Under the decree system, they were liberated and most of them became civilians.<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,百科事典マイペディア,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル大辞泉,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,世界大百科事典 |title=品部(ともべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%93%81%E9%83%A8-74567 |access-date=2023-01-29 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
According to legend, they descended from [[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |date=24 February 2006 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenoakarutama |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9437 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211024102555/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9437 |archive-date=24 October 2021 |access-date=19 December 2020 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]]}}</ref>
== History == In the pre-Taika period, they were led by powerful clans such as the Banzo, and provided various goods and labor to the Imperial Court (Yamato Kingdom). It is also said that many of the groups of engineers who came to Japan after the latter half of the [[5th century]] belonged to the Shinabe clan. After the [[Taika Reform]], some of them were abolished, but the rest were reorganized and attached to government officials, who were obliged to produce luxury goods for the court and industrial products requiring special technology as part of tribute. In the staff ordinances within the [[Taihō Code|Taihō ordinances]], the affiliated government offices, the name of the product department, and the legal number of units were each defined as follows.
* Library - paper door (50) * Gagaku-ryo - Gagaku (49), Kioto (8), Nara-fueki (9) * Zouheiji - Zoukou - Zoukoudo...Tsume-kou (18), Tatehou (36), Bunbaku (16) * Drum-blowers - drum-blowing doors (large angle blowers) (218) * Chusenji - Funado (Funamorido) (100) * Main falconer - falconer's door (falconry door) (17) * Okura-sho - Komainoto...Oshikaido Komainoto (5), Takeshido Komainoto (7), Murakami Komainoto (30), Miyagun Komainoto (14), Dai Komainome (6), Koromoome (21), Asuka Kutsumi (12), Kuretokosaku (2), Futaumi (11), Okasami (33), Suddenly Saku (72) * Lacquer Master - Lacquer Department...Lacquer (15), Claywork (10), Leatherwork (4) * Weaving: Nishiki Twill Weaving (110), Kofuku Department (7), Kawauchi Kunihiro Silk Weavers, etc. (350), Scarlet Dyeing (70), Indigo Dyeing (33) * [[Ōzenshoku]] - Zoukido...Ukai (37), Ejin (87), Amibiki (150), Mishou (20) * [[Ōiryō|Otsukuryo]]-Otsukurido (25) * [[Ōiryō|Otakiryo]]-Yakudo (75), Nyudo (50) * [[Zōzakeji]]-Sakeido (185) * Sonokoji - Sonoto (300) * Earthworks - mud (51) * Shusuiji - Hido (Mito) (114)
As for their status, they were considered to be good citizens like ordinary citizens (peasants), but their status was slightly inferior to that of ordinary citizens. However, they were less subordinate to the government, and were registered in the family register of their place of residence on the same basis as ordinary citizens, so there was almost no discrimination in treatment between them and ordinary citizens. In addition to the regular members who inherited their duties and status, there were also temporary members who were incorporated from the general public. In other cases, one person per household either worked in shifts for a certain period of time, or was temporarily assigned by the government to perform labor or pay a certain amount of tribute. In exchange, they were exempted from some or all of their household duties and were also excluded from military service.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
In the [[Nara period]], in [[Yōrō]] 5 ([[721]]), the chief hawks were abolished, and in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 3 ([[759]]), all hinto except for highly skilled "sekkyo-sōdensha" were, in principle, abolished and incorporated into the public domain. In [[759]], in principle, Shinto houses other than those for highly skilled "sekkyo-soden" were abolished and incorporated into public houses (treated as ordinary citizens). In the [[Heian period]], only the Kofukishi of the Hyogo Dormitory were included in the [[Engishiki|Enkishiki]]. This is thought to have been due to a complicated combination of factors: the fact that the old tribal system itself was incompatible with the Ritsuryo system; the fact that the decline of the Ritsuryo system made it difficult to maintain the tribal system; and the fact that the improvement of socioeconomic standards made it possible to procure and hire services from the private sector (in this case, the private sector included those procured from the former tribal merchants and traders). In this case, the private sector included the commercial and industrial sectors of the former Shinbu.{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}
In academic circles, it is sometimes referred to as ''Shinabe Zakkosei'' together with the similar organization of Zakkosei, but Zakkosei were engaged in military-related technical labor and had their own family registers that were different from the general family registers, and the legal status difference between them and the public was stronger than that of Shinabe. The legal status difference between them and the public is said to be stronger than that of the Shinabe.
== Clans ==
=== {{Anchor|Fuhibote|Fuhito clan}}Fuhito clan === The Fuhitobe ('''史部)''', were a caste in [[Japan]] during the [[Kofun period]]. They were a [[Shinabe clan]] who were engaged in duties related to writing at the [[Yamato Kingship|Yamato Imperial Court]]. Most of them were thought to be descended from [[Toraijin]] or people who came to Japan from the continent or peninsula. Unlike the general nobles, they were given the family name "Fuhito" (史).<ref name="字通世界大百科事典内言及-2023a">{{Cite web |last=字通,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),山川 日本史小辞典 改訂新版,世界大百科事典 第２版,普及版 |title=史部(ふびとべ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8F%B2%E9%83%A8-620106 |access-date=2023-12-07 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
The duties of the Fuhitobe included taking charge of records and documents during the period before the [[Taika (era)|Taika era]] (618-867).<ref name="字通世界大百科事典内言及-2023a" />
In the Chinese context, the term "Shibu" of the same Kanji ('''史部)''' is also used to refer to one of the four divisions of literature, known as the Shi division, which includes historical books and geography.<ref name="字通世界大百科事典内言及-2023a" /> <ref>{{Cite web |title="史部"包括哪些典籍？ – 国学网 |url=http://www.guoxue.com/?p=221 |access-date=2023-01-27 |language=zh-CN}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Kikuo Arai, "Shinabe" ("Encyclopedia of Japanese History 3" (Heibonsha, 1993) ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1) * Masaaki Ueda, "Shinabe, Zodo" (Encyclopedia of Social Sciences 14 (Kajima Institute Press, 1974) ISBN 978-4-306-09165-8) * {{Cite book|author=Jinno Seiichi|title=Genealogy of the View of Low and Highborn|date=1997-02-01|publisher={{Ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}} |isbn=4-642-05408-1|series=History and Culture Library|volume=8|location =[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language=Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jino}}}} {{Cite book|author=Jino Seiichi| title=Genealogy of the Low Countries View|date=1997-02-01|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|isbn=4-642-05408-1|series=History and Culture Library|volume=8|location=[[Tokyo]], [[Bunkyo-ku]]|language= Japanese|ref={{SfnRef|Jono}}}} * Kimio Kumagai, "Pinabe" and "Pinabe and Zodo System" in Dictionary of National History 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986) ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4

#redirect[[en:Shinabe clans]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]
#redirect[[en:Shinabe clans]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]]
#redirect[[en:Shinabe clans]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]]

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[Japan|{{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}]] that carried the [[Wakun|{{ill|Wakun|ja|和訓}}]] readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[Beminsei|{{ill|Beminsei|ja|部民制}}]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[Beminsei|{{ill|Bemin|ja|部民制}}]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[Taika Reform|{{ill|Taika|ja|大化}}]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including [[Nashiro|{{ill|Nashiro|ja|名代}}]]. * After the [[Taika Reform|{{ill|Taika|ja|大化}}]] and during the [[Ritsuryō system|{{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}}]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the [[Tomobe|{{ill|Tomobe|ja|伴造}}]] and served the Yamato court ([[Yamato polity|{{ill|Yamato Ōken|ja|ヤマト王権}}]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[Toraijin|{{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}}]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[Taika Reform|{{ill|Taika no Kaishin|ja|大化の改新}}]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[Taihō Code|{{ill|Taihō Ritsuryō|ja|大宝律令}}]], the [[Shikinenryō|{{ill|Shikinenryō|ja|職員令}}]] defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* [[Toshoryō|{{ill|Toshoryō|ja|図書寮}}]] - Paper households (50) * [[Gagakuryō|{{ill|Gagakuryō|ja|雅楽寮}}]] - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * [[Zōheishi|{{ill|Zōheishi|ja|造兵司}}]] - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * [[Kobukushi|{{ill|Kobukushi|ja|鼓吹司}}]] - [[Kobukuto|{{ill|Kobukuto|ja|鼓吹戸}}]] (Ōtsunebue) (218) * [[Shusen-shi|{{ill|Shusen-shi|ja|主船司}}]] - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * [[Shuyō-shi|{{ill|Shuyō-shi|ja|主鷹司}}]] - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[Ōkura-shō|{{ill|Ōkura-shō|ja|大蔵省 (律令制)}}]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * [[Urushibe-shi|{{ill|Urushibe-shi|ja|漆部司}}]] - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * [[Oribebe-shi|{{ill|Oribebe-shi|ja|織部司}}]] - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * [[Daizenshiki|{{ill|Daizenshiki|ja|大膳職}}]] - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[Ōikeryō|{{ill|Ōikeryō|ja|大炊寮}}]] - Cooking households (25) * [[Ten'yakuryō|{{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮}}]] - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * [[Sakabe-shi|{{ill|Sakabe-shi|ja|造酒司}}]] - Sake households (185) * [[Enchishi|{{ill|Enchishi|ja|園池司}}]] - Garden households (300) * [[Dokoshi|{{ill|Dokoshi|ja|土工司}}]] - Mud households (51) * [[Shusui-shi|{{ill|Shusui-shi|ja|主水司}}]] - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[Nara period|{{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}]], in the 5th year of [[Yōrō|{{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}}]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[Tempō Hōji|{{ill|Tempō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}}]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[Heian period|{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}]], only the Kobukushi of the [[Hyōgoryō|{{ill|Hyōgoryō|ja|兵庫寮}}]] remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[Ritsuryō system|{{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes [[Zakko|{{ill|Zakko|ja|雑戸}}]]. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|ja|日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|ja|和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Beminsei|ja|部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|ja|部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|ja|大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|ja|名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|ja|大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.

Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:

In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
