[[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kk]] [[ja:品部]]

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
== References == {{Reflist|18em}}
== Related articles == * {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} * {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}} * {{ill|lt=Kannuhi|1=Kannuhi|2=ja|3=官奴婢}}

They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.

Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:

Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

revision.text:
'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
== References == {{Reflist|18em}}
== Related articles == * {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} * {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}} * {{ill|lt=Kannuhi|1=Kannuhi|2=ja|3=官奴婢}}

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
== References == {{Reflist|18em}}
== Related articles == * {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} * {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}} * {{ill|lt=Kannuhi|1=Kannuhi|2=ja|3=官奴婢}}

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)

After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:

There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:

Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.

After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. * {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
