After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

revision.text:
'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|12=draft}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|12=draft}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|12=draft}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|12=draft}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|12=draft}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|12=draft}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|12=draft}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|12=draft}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|12=draft}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|12=draft}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|12=draft}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|12=draft}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=draft}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|12=draft}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=draft}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=draft}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|12=draft}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|12=draft}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|12=draft}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|12=draft}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|12=draft}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=draft}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|12=draft}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=draft}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本|12=draft}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|12=draft}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|12=draft}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|12=draft}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|12=draft}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|12=draft}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|12=draft}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制|12=draft}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|12=draft}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|12=draft}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人|12=draft}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新|12=draft}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|12=draft}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|12=draft}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|12=draft}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|12=draft}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|12=draft}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|12=draft}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|12=draft}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|12=draft}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|12=draft}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|12=draft}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|12=draft}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|12=draft}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|12=draft}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|12=draft}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=draft}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|12=draft}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=draft}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=draft}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|12=draft}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|12=draft}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|12=draft}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|12=draft}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|12=draft}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=draft}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|12=draft}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=draft}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

{{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese institutional history]] [[Category:Bemin]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:pages tagged as Automatic wikipedia redirects in namespace ]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Shinabe clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Shinabe clans]] [[sn:hinabe clans]] [[ja:品部]]

'''品部'''（しなべ/ともべ）とは、「しなのとものを」あるいは「とものみやつこ」の[[和訓]]を持つ[[古代]][[日本]]の人的集団・組織のこと。[[部民制]]の後の制度。
== 概要 == *複数の[[部民制|部]]あるいは部全体の総称。 *[[大化]]前代においては、朝廷に直属する部民（職業部・[[名代]]）のこと。 *大化以後及び[[律令制]]において[[官司]]に配属されて宮中で用いる物資の生産・技術伝習にあたった人々。大化前代の[[部民制|職業部]]の後身とされる。
上記のように複数の意味を有する。
大化前代においては、[[伴造]]などの豪族に率いられて朝廷（[[ヤマト王権]]）に様々な物資あるいは労働力の形で奉仕を行った。また、[[5世紀]]後半以後に渡来した[[渡来人]]の技術者集団の中には品部に属した者が多いとされている。[[大化の改新]]以後、一部は廃止されるが残りは再編されて官司に付属され、貢納の一環として宮廷で用いる奢侈品や特殊な技術を必要とする工業製品の生産を義務付けられるようになる。[[大宝律令|大宝令]]内の[[職員令]]においては、所属官庁、品部名、法定戸数はそれぞれ以下のように定められていた。
*[[図書寮]]－紙戸（50） *[[雅楽寮]]－楽戸…伎楽（49）・木登（8）・奈良笛吹（9） *[[造兵司]]－雑工戸…爪工（18）・楯縫（36）・幄作（16） *[[鼓吹司]]－[[鼓吹戸]]（大角吹）（218） *[[主船司]]－船戸（船守戸）（100） *[[主鷹司]]－鷹戸（鷹養戸）（17） *[[大蔵省 (律令制)|大蔵省]]－狛戸…忍海戸狛人（5）・竹志戸狛人（7）・村々狛人（30）・宮郡狛人（14）・大狛染（6）・衣染（21）・飛鳥沓縫（12）・呉床作（2）・蓋縫（11）・大笠縫（33）・桉作（72） *[[漆部司]]－漆部…漆部（15）・泥障（10）・革張（4） *[[織部司]]－染戸…錦綾織（110）・呉服部（7）・川内国広絹織人等（350）・緋染（70）・藍染（33） *[[大膳職]]－雑供戸…鵜飼（37）・江人（87）・網引（150）・未醤（20） *[[大炊寮]]－大炊戸（25） *[[典薬寮]]－薬戸（75）・乳戸（50） *[[造酒司]]－酒戸（185） *[[園池司]]－園戸（300） *[[土工司]]－泥戸（51） *[[主水司]]－氷戸（水戸）（114）
身分としては、一般の公民（百姓）と同じ良民を構成したが、公民と比べると身分は若干劣るものとされた。ただし、官庁への隷属度は弱く、戸籍登録も居住地の戸籍に一般公民と等しく登録されるなど、待遇としては一般公民との差別はほぼ無かったとされる。職務・身分を世襲する'''常の品部'''のほかに、一般公民より臨時に編入する'''借（かり）の品部'''が存在した。狛戸及び染戸の一部は毎年一定量の料物を貢納したが、その他は1戸あたり1名が一定期間の交替制か臨時に官司に上番して労役を行うか一定量の製品を貢納した。その代わりとして戸内の課役の一部もしくは全てが免除され、兵役からも除外された{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
[[奈良時代]]に入ると、[[養老]]5年（[[721年]]）に主鷹司の品部廃止が行われ、続いて[[天平宝字]]3年（[[759年]]）に高度な技術を要する「世業相伝者」以外の品戸は原則廃止されて公戸に編入（一般の公民扱い）された。その後も品戸の廃止が続き、[[平安時代]]の『[[延喜式]]』の時点では兵庫寮の鼓吹司のみとなった。これは古い部民制の残滓である品部そのものが律令制と相容れなかったこと、律令制の衰退によって品部の維持が困難になってきたこと、社会経済水準の向上によって民間からの調達・雇役が可能になったこと（この場合の民間には旧品部の商工業者からの調達を含む）が複雑に絡み合っていたと考えられている{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
学術的には同様の組織である[[雑戸]]と合わせて'''品部・雑戸制'''（しなべ・ざっこせい）とよぶ場合もあるが、雑戸の職掌範囲は軍事関係の技術労働に従事しており、またその戸籍は一般の戸籍とは違う[[雑戸籍]]が独自に構成されており、公民との法的な身分差は品部よりも強いとされる{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}。

== 関連項目 == *[[部民制]] *[[雑戸]] *[[官奴婢]]
{{Japanese-history-stub|しなへ}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:しなへ}} [[Category:古墳時代]] [[Category:飛鳥時代]] [[Category:奈良時代]] [[Category:日本の制度史 (古代)]] [[Category:部民|*しなへ]]

'''品部'''（しなべ/ともべ）とは、「しなのとものを」あるいは「とものみやつこ」の[[和訓]]を持つ[[古代]][[日本]]の人的集団・組織のこと。[[部民制]]の後の制度。
== 概要 == *複数の[[部民制|部]]あるいは部全体の総称。 *[[大化]]前代においては、朝廷に直属する部民（職業部・[[名代]]）のこと。 *大化以後及び[[律令制]]において[[官司]]に配属されて宮中で用いる物資の生産・技術伝習にあたった人々。大化前代の[[部民制|職業部]]の後身とされる。
上記のように複数の意味を有する。
大化前代においては、[[伴造]]などの豪族に率いられて朝廷（[[ヤマト王権]]）に様々な物資あるいは労働力の形で奉仕を行った。また、[[5世紀]]後半以後に渡来した[[渡来人]]の技術者集団の中には品部に属した者が多いとされている。[[大化の改新]]以後、一部は廃止されるが残りは再編されて官司に付属され、貢納の一環として宮廷で用いる奢侈品や特殊な技術を必要とする工業製品の生産を義務付けられるようになる。[[大宝律令|大宝令]]内の[[職員令]]においては、所属官庁、品部名、法定戸数はそれぞれ以下のように定められていた。
*[[図書寮]]－紙戸（50） *[[雅楽寮]]－楽戸…伎楽（49）・木登（8）・奈良笛吹（9） *[[造兵司]]－雑工戸…爪工（18）・楯縫（36）・幄作（16） *[[鼓吹司]]－[[鼓吹戸]]（大角吹）（218） *[[主船司]]－船戸（船守戸）（100） *[[主鷹司]]－鷹戸（鷹養戸）（17） *[[大蔵省 (律令制)|大蔵省]]－狛戸…忍海戸狛人（5）・竹志戸狛人（7）・村々狛人（30）・宮郡狛人（14）・大狛染（6）・衣染（21）・飛鳥沓縫（12）・呉床作（2）・蓋縫（11）・大笠縫（33）・桉作（72） *[[漆部司]]－漆部…漆部（15）・泥障（10）・革張（4） *[[織部司]]－染戸…錦綾織（110）・呉服部（7）・川内国広絹織人等（350）・緋染（70）・藍染（33） *[[大膳職]]－雑供戸…鵜飼（37）・江人（87）・網引（150）・未醤（20） *[[大炊寮]]－大炊戸（25） *[[典薬寮]]－薬戸（75）・乳戸（50） *[[造酒司]]－酒戸（185） *[[園池司]]－園戸（300） *[[土工司]]－泥戸（51） *[[主水司]]－氷戸（水戸）（114）
身分としては、一般の公民（百姓）と同じ良民を構成したが、公民と比べると身分は若干劣るものとされた。ただし、官庁への隷属度は弱く、戸籍登録も居住地の戸籍に一般公民と等しく登録されるなど、待遇としては一般公民との差別はほぼ無かったとされる。職務・身分を世襲する'''常の品部'''のほかに、一般公民より臨時に編入する'''借（かり）の品部'''が存在した。狛戸及び染戸の一部は毎年一定量の料物を貢納したが、その他は1戸あたり1名が一定期間の交替制か臨時に官司に上番して労役を行うか一定量の製品を貢納した。その代わりとして戸内の課役の一部もしくは全てが免除され、兵役からも除外された{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
[[奈良時代]]に入ると、[[養老]]5年（[[721年]]）に主鷹司の品部廃止が行われ、続いて[[天平宝字]]3年（[[759年]]）に高度な技術を要する「世業相伝者」以外の品戸は原則廃止されて公戸に編入（一般の公民扱い）された。その後も品戸の廃止が続き、[[平安時代]]の『[[延喜式]]』の時点では兵庫寮の鼓吹司のみとなった。これは古い部民制の残滓である品部そのものが律令制と相容れなかったこと、律令制の衰退によって品部の維持が困難になってきたこと、社会経済水準の向上によって民間からの調達・雇役が可能になったこと（この場合の民間には旧品部の商工業者からの調達を含む）が複雑に絡み合っていたと考えられている{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}。
学術的には同様の組織である[[雑戸]]と合わせて'''品部・雑戸制'''（しなべ・ざっこせい）とよぶ場合もあるが、雑戸の職掌範囲は軍事関係の技術労働に従事しており、またその戸籍は一般の戸籍とは違う[[雑戸籍]]が独自に構成されており、公民との法的な身分差は品部よりも強いとされる{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}。

{{Japanese-history-stub|しなへ}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:しなへ}} [[Category:部民|*しなへ]] [[Category:古墳時代]] [[Category:飛鳥時代]] [[Category:奈良時代]]

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本|12=draft}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|12=draft}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|12=draft}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|12=draft}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|12=draft}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|12=draft}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|12=draft}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制|12=draft}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|12=draft}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|12=draft}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人|12=draft}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新|12=draft}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|12=draft}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|12=draft}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|12=draft}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|12=draft}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|12=draft}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|12=draft}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|12=draft}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|12=draft}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|12=draft}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|12=draft}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|12=draft}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|12=draft}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|12=draft}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|12=draft}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=draft}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|12=draft}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=draft}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=draft}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|12=draft}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|12=draft}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|12=draft}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|12=draft}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|12=draft}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=draft}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|12=draft}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=draft}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
== References == {{Reflist|18em}}

Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|12=draft}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|12=draft}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|12=draft}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|12=draft}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|12=draft}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|12=draft}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|12=draft}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|12=draft}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|12=draft}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|12=draft}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|12=draft}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|12=draft}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|12=draft}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|12=draft}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=draft}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|12=draft}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=draft}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=draft}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|12=draft}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|12=draft}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|12=draft}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|12=draft}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|12=draft}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=draft}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|12=draft}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=draft}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

revision.text:
'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本|12=draft}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|12=draft}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|12=draft}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|12=draft}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|12=draft}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|12=draft}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|12=draft}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制|12=draft}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|12=draft}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|12=draft}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人|12=draft}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新|12=draft}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|12=draft}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|12=draft}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|12=draft}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|12=draft}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|12=draft}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|12=draft}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|12=draft}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|12=draft}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|12=draft}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|12=draft}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|12=draft}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|12=draft}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|12=draft}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|12=draft}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=draft}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|12=draft}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=draft}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=draft}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|12=draft}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|12=draft}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|12=draft}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|12=draft}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|12=draft}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=draft}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|12=draft}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=draft}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
== References == {{Reflist|18em}}
== Related articles == * {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|lt=Kannuhi|1=Kannuhi|2=ja|3=官奴婢|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ōkura-shō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkura-shō|qq=}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ōikeryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōikeryō|qq=}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nara period|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara period|qq=}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Yōrō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tempō Hōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Tempō Hōji|qq=}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Heian period|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ritsuryō system|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|qq=}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

<!--Shinabe clans-->

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Bemin system|13=User:Immanuelle/Bemin|qq=}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Beminsei|13=User:Immanuelle/Bemin|qq=}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taika Reform|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika|qq=}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taika Reform|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika|qq=}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ritsuryō system|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Yamato polity|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōken|qq=}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toraijin|13=User:Immanuelle/Toraijin|qq=}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taika Reform|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika no Kaishin|qq=}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taihō Code|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Ritsuryō|qq=}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ōkura-shō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkura-shō|qq=}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ōikeryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōikeryō|qq=}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nara period|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara period|qq=}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Yōrō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tempō Hōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Tempō Hōji|qq=}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Heian period|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ritsuryō system|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|qq=}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Yamato polity|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōken|qq=}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toraijin|13=User:Immanuelle/Toraijin|qq=}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taika Reform|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika no Kaishin|qq=}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taihō Code|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Ritsuryō|qq=}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ōkura-shō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkura-shō|qq=}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ōikeryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōikeryō|qq=}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nara period|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara period|qq=}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Yōrō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tempō Hōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Tempō Hōji|qq=}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Heian period|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ritsuryō system|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|qq=}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
