'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Bemin system|13=User:Immanuelle/Bemin|qq=}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Beminsei|13=User:Immanuelle/Bemin|qq=}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taika Reform|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika|qq=}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taika Reform|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika|qq=}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ritsuryō system|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Yamato polity|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōken|qq=}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toraijin|13=User:Immanuelle/Toraijin|qq=}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taika Reform|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika no Kaishin|qq=}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taihō Code|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Ritsuryō|qq=}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ōkura-shō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkura-shō|qq=}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ōikeryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōikeryō|qq=}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nara period|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara period|qq=}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Yōrō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tempō Hōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Tempō Hōji|qq=}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Heian period|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ritsuryō system|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|qq=}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient {{ill|lt=Japan|1=Japan|2=ja|3=日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}} that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the {{ill|lt=Bemin system|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Bemin system|13=User:Immanuelle/Bemin|qq=}}.
== Overview == * A general term for multiple {{ill|lt=Beminsei|1=Bemin|2=ja|3=部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Beminsei|13=User:Immanuelle/Bemin|qq=}} groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taika Reform|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika|qq=}}, it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=}}. * After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika|2=ja|3=大化|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taika Reform|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika|qq=}} and during the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ritsuryō system|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=}} and served the Yamato court ({{ill|lt=Yamato polity|1=Yamato Ōken|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Yamato polity|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōken|qq=}}) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the {{ill|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin|2=ja|3=渡来人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toraijin|13=User:Immanuelle/Toraijin|qq=}}, who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the {{ill|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika no Kaishin|2=ja|3=大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taika Reform|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika no Kaishin|qq=}}, some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the {{ill|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Ritsuryō|2=ja|3=大宝律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Taihō Code|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Ritsuryō|qq=}}, the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * {{ill|lt=Ōkura-shō|1=Ōkura-shō|2=ja|3=大蔵省 (律令制)|1=Ministry of the Treasury|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ōkura-shō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkura-shō|qq=}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * {{ill|lt=Ōikeryō|1=Ōikeryō|2=ja|3=大炊寮|1=Ōiryō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ōikeryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōikeryō|qq=}} - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the {{ill|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period|2=ja|3=奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nara period|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara period|qq=}}, in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō|2=ja|3=養老|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Yōrō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}} (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Tempō Hōji|1=Tempō Hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tempō Hōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Tempō Hōji|qq=}} (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the {{ill|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period|2=ja|3=平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Heian period|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō system|2=ja|3=律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ritsuryō system|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|qq=}}, the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
== References == {{Reflist|18em}}
== Related articles == * [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]] * {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}} * {{ill|lt=Kannuhi|1=Kannuhi|2=ja|3=官奴婢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kannuhi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannuhi|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|品部|version=104426480|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[sn:Shinabe clans]][[sn:Shinabe clans]]}}

Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Expanded pages from Wikipedia
revision.text:
'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
== References == {{Reflist|18em}}
== Related articles == * [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]] * {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}} * {{ill|lt=Kannuhi|1=Kannuhi|2=ja|3=官奴婢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kannuhi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannuhi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{translated page|ja|品部|version=104426480|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

revision.comment: Bot: remove obsolete category Expanded Pages from Wikipedia
revision.text:
'''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
== References == {{Reflist|18em}}
== Related articles == * [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]] * {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}} * {{ill|lt=Kannuhi|1=Kannuhi|2=ja|3=官奴婢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kannuhi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannuhi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{translated page|ja|品部|version=104426480|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<noinclude></noinclude>
[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[sn:Shinabe clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]]
[[sn:Shinabe clans]][[sn:Shinabe clans]]}}

Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11418456]] Wikidata name: shinabe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=Wakun|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomobe|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shikinenryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Toshoryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagakuryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=}} - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Daizenshiki|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Sakabe-shi|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=Zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11418456]] Wikidata name: shinabe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=kun'yomi|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1147749}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11414721}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomomiyatsuko|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381545}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shokuinryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60996852}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Zusho-ryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420348}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagaku-ryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8963315}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310428}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310430}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368805}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} -
