Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11422804}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423446}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Shusui-shi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=shusuishi|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11418456]] Wikidata name: shinabe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=kun'yomi|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1147749}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11414721}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomomiyatsuko|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381545}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shokuinryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60996852}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Zusho-ryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420348}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagaku-ryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8963315}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310428}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368805}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Main Water Office|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinabe clans]][[ja:品部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11418456]] Wikidata name: shinabe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=kun'yomi|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1147749}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11414721}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomomiyatsuko|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381545}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shokuinryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60996852}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Zusho-ryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420348}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagaku-ryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8963315}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639728}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310428}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310430}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368805}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17226200}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Dazai Gon no Daibu|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438575}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Brewing Department|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639771}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11422804}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423446}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Main Water Office|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.
== References == {{Reflist|18em}}
== Related articles == * [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]] * {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}} * {{ill|lt=Kannuhi|1=Kannuhi|2=ja|3=官奴婢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kannuhi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannuhi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{translated page|ja|品部|version=104426480|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310430}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinabe clans]][[ja:品部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11418456]] Wikidata name: shinabe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=kun'yomi|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1147749}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11414721}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomomiyatsuko|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381545}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shokuinryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60996852}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Zusho-ryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420348}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagaku-ryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8963315}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639728}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310428}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310430}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368805}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17226200}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Dazai Gon no Daibu|2=ja|3=大膳職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438575}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Brewing Department|2=ja|3=造酒司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639771}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11422804}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423446}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Main Water Office|2=ja|3=主水司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinabe clans]][[ja:品部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11418456]] Wikidata name: shinabe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=kun'yomi|2=ja|3=和訓|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1147749}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11414721}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomomiyatsuko|2=ja|3=伴造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381545}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shokuinryō|2=ja|3=職員令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60996852}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:

Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17226200}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Dazai Gon no Daibu|2=ja|3=大膳職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438575}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Brewing Department|2=ja|3=造酒司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639771}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11422804}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423446}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Main Water Office|2=ja|3=主水司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinabe clans]][[ja:品部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11418456]] Wikidata name: shinabe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=kun'yomi|2=ja|3=和訓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1147749}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11414721}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomomiyatsuko|2=ja|3=伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381545}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shokuinryō|2=ja|3=職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60996852}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Zusho-ryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420348}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagaku-ryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8963315}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639728}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310428}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310430}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368805}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17226200}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Dazai Gon no Daibu|2=ja|3=大膳職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438575}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Brewing Department|2=ja|3=造酒司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639771}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11422804}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423446}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Main Water Office|2=ja|3=主水司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinabe clans]][[ja:品部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11418456]] Wikidata name: shinabe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=kun'yomi|2=ja|3=和訓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1147749}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11414721}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomomiyatsuko|2=ja|3=伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381545}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shokuinryō|2=ja|3=職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60996852}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Zusho-ryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420348}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagaku-ryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8963315}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639728}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310428}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310430}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368805}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17226200}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Dazai Gon no Daibu|2=ja|3=大膳職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438575}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Brewing Department|2=ja|3=造酒司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639771}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11422804}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423446}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Main Water Office|2=ja|3=主水司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shokuinryō|2=ja|3=職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60996852}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Zusho-ryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420348}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagaku-ryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8963315}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639728}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310428}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310430}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368805}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17226200}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Dazai Gon no Daibu|2=ja|3=大膳職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438575}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Brewing Department|2=ja|3=造酒司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639771}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11422804}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423446}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Main Water Office|2=ja|3=主水司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinabe clans]][[ja:品部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11418456]] Wikidata name: shinabe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=kun'yomi|2=ja|3=和訓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1147749}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11414721}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomomiyatsuko|2=ja|3=伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381545}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shokuinryō|2=ja|3=職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60996852}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Zusho-ryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420348}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagaku-ryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8963315}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639728}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310428}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310430}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Ship households (Ship maintenance households) (100) * {{ill|lt=Shuyō-shi|1=Shuyō-shi|2=ja|3=主鷹司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shuyō-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuyō-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368805}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Lacquer workers: Lacquer workers (15), Mud wall decorators (10), Leather craftsmen (4) * {{ill|lt=Oribebe-shi|1=Oribebe-shi|2=ja|3=織部司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Oribebe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Oribebe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17226200}} - Dyeing households: Brocade weavers (110), Wu textile artisans (7), Kawachi silk weavers (350), Scarlet dyers (70), Indigo dyers (33) * {{ill|lt=Daizenshiki|1=Dazai Gon no Daibu|2=ja|3=大膳職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Daizenshiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Daizenshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438575}} - Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Medicine households (75), Milk households (50) * {{ill|lt=Sakabe-shi|1=Brewing Department|2=ja|3=造酒司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sakabe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakabe-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639771}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11422804}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423446}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Main Water Office|2=ja|3=主水司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

Miscellaneous supply households: Fishermen (37), River people (87), Net makers (150), Soy sauce brewers (20) * [[:en:Ōiryō|Ōikeryō]] - Cooking households (25) * {{ill|lt=Ten'yakuryō|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}} - Sake households (185) * {{ill|lt=Enchishi|1=Enchishi|2=ja|3=園池司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Enchishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11422804}} - Garden households (300) * {{ill|lt=Dokoshi|1=Dokoshi|2=ja|3=土工司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Dokoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Dokoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423446}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. Some groups, such as Koma households and dyeing households, had annual tribute quotas, while others rotated members for service or supplied goods. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained. The decline was due to the incompatibility of Shinabe with the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the decline of that system, and the rise of private industry, including former Shinabe artisans{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
Scholars sometimes refer to a combined system called '''Shinabe-Zakko System''' (品部・雑戸制) that includes {{ill|lt=Zakko|1=zakko|2=ja|3=雑戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zakko|13=User:Immanuelle/Zakko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10331887}}. However, Zakko had a distinct military role, was registered in a separate Zakko census, and had a stronger legal distinction from commoners{{Sfn|神野|p=15}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinabe clans]][[ja:品部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinabe clans]][[ja:品部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11418456]] Wikidata name: shinabe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shinabe''' (品部) refers to a group or organization of people in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]] that carried the {{ill|lt=Wakun|1=kun'yomi|2=ja|3=和訓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Wakun|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 訓読み|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1147749}} readings "shina no tomo no wo" or "tomo no miyatsuko." It was a system that followed the [[:en:Bemin|Bemin system]].
== Overview == * A general term for multiple [[:en:Bemin|Beminsei]] groups or the entirety of such groups. * Before the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]], it referred to occupational groups directly attached to the court, including {{ill|lt=Nashiro|1=Nashiro|2=ja|3=名代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Nashiro|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11414721}}. * After the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika Reform]] and during the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it referred to those assigned to government offices to produce goods and pass down technical skills for use in the imperial court. They were considered the successors of the occupational groups of the pre-Taika period.
Shinabe had multiple meanings as described above.
Before the Taika Reform, they were led by powerful families like the {{ill|lt=Tomobe|1=Tomomiyatsuko|2=ja|3=伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Tomobe|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381545}} and served the Yamato court ([[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato polity]]) by providing various goods and labor. Many artisans among the [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]], who arrived in Japan after the latter half of the 5th century, were also classified as Shinabe. After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], some were abolished, while others were reorganized under government offices and required to produce luxury goods and specialized industrial products for the court as part of taxation. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]], the {{ill|lt=Shikinenryō|1=Shokuinryō|2=ja|3=職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shikinenryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60996852}} defined their affiliation, names, and legal household numbers as follows:
* {{ill|lt=Toshoryō|1=Zusho-ryō|2=ja|3=図書寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Toshoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420348}} - Paper households (50) * {{ill|lt=Gagakuryō|1=Gagaku-ryō|2=ja|3=雅楽寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Gagakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagakuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8963315}} - Music households: Gigaku (49), Wooden acrobats (8), Nara flutists (9) * {{ill|lt=Zōheishi|1=Zōheishi|2=ja|3=造兵司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Zōheishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōheishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11639728}} - Miscellaneous artisans: Nail craftsmen (18), Shield makers (36), Tent makers (16) * {{ill|lt=Kobukushi|1=Kobukushi|2=ja|3=鼓吹司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310428}} - {{ill|lt=Kobukuto|1=Kobukuto|2=ja|3=鼓吹戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Kobukuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobukuto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97310430}} (Ōtsunebue) (218) * {{ill|lt=Shusen-shi|1=Shusen-shi|2=ja|3=主船司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusen-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusen-shi|qq=}} - Falcon households (Falconers) (17) * [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ōkura-shō]] - Koma households: Onukai Koma people (5), Takeshi Koma people (7), Village Koma people (30), Miyagori Koma people (14), Large Koma dyers (6), Cloth dyers (21), Asuka shoemakers (12), Wu bed makers (2), Cover seamers (11), Large kasa seamers (33), Tatami makers (72) * {{ill|lt=Urushibe-shi|1=Urushibe-shi|2=ja|3=漆部司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Urushibe-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Urushibe-shi|qq=}} - Mud households (51) * {{ill|lt=Shusui-shi|1=Main Water Office|2=ja|3=主水司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Shusui-shi|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusui-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368771}} - Ice households (Water households) (114)
Their status was equal to that of ordinary public citizens (百姓, "hyakushō"), but they were considered slightly lower in Rank. However, they had less strict government servitude, were registered in local census records as commoners, and were generally not treated differently. There were two types: hereditary '''Permanent Shinabe''' and temporary '''Borrowed Shinabe''' recruited from commoners. In return, they received tax exemptions, including exemption from military service{{Sfn|神野|pp=15-17}}.
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], in the 5th year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (721), the Shinabe of the Shuyō-shi were abolished, followed by a mass abolition in the 3rd year of [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tempō Hōji]] (759), except for those with hereditary expertise, who were reclassified as commoners. Shinabe continued to be abolished, and by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only the Kobukushi of the {{ill|lt=Hyōgoryō|1=Hyōgoryō|2=ja|3=兵庫寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Hyōgoryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgoryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392842}} remained.
